title: Podman in a Debian container on AWS Lambda fails on multiple commands
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
podman 3.0.1 installed though Debian's official repos onto a Debian Bullseye container running in AWS Lambda throws a `Error: no such file or directory` when running any command other than some simple ones like `podman --version` or `podman --help`

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. Create a Docker image with podman on it. Basically follow this - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/images-create.html#images-create-from-alt
but use python:3.9-bullseye as the base image and install podman - `RUN apt-get update -y && apt-get upgrade -y && apt-get install -y podman`

2. In python, try to execute something like `subprocess.run(f"podman login -u {username} -p {password} {registry} --authfile /tmp/auth.json")`
I am using a random file in /tmp as in AWS Lambda only /tmp is writable. I wonder if that has something to do with the issue... Does podman try to create any other files it does not find? Or maybe it writes to existing read-only files created during image creation outside of /tmp?

3. Run that in Lambda (running the container locally succeeds)

**Describe the results you received:**
`Error: no such file or directory`

**Describe the results you expected:**
`Login successful!`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
Restrictions for running a container in a Lambda (where I suspect podman breaks the rules in its default configuration) - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/images-create.html#images-reqs
Mainly worried about the read-only file system restriction, what do we need to set to be in /tmp for the use cases of logging into a repo, pulling an image and doing a podman cp?

**Output of `podman version`:

```
3.0.1
```

**Output of `podman info`:
should be irrelevant for doing a login, right?

**Successful run in container locally with log level = debug:**


**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**
Did not test the latest version as there is no easy way to install the latest version of Podman on Debian. Nothing seen on the troubleshooting guide.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Please try the latest podman versions (v4.3 or v4.2 at least), we cannot support such old versions upstream.
Do I then have to manually build podman for Debian?
https://podman.io/getting-started/installation look under the debian section, there is a experimental repo.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@petarlishov did it work with the latest version?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we never heard back, I am assuming the answer was yes.
